items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.title: Understanding Primary Accidental Hypothermia: Causes and Vulnerable Populations
items.content: Primary accidental hypothermia results from direct exposure to cold in previously healthy individuals. It is prevalent across seasons and climates, with higher vulnerability in the extremes of age—elderly and neonates—due to factors like diminished thermal perception, immobility, malnutrition, and systemic illnesses.
keyPoints: Accidental hypothermia occurs when core temperature drops below 35°C (95°F).
Elderly have diminished thermal perception; neonates have high heat loss due to increased surface-to-mass ratio.
Malnutrition contributes to heat loss through reduced subcutaneous fat and depleted energy stores for thermogenesis.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.title: Risk Factors and Mechanisms of Heat Loss in Hypothermia
items.content: Occupational exposure to cold, certain medications, and endocrine dysfunctions are significant risk factors for hypothermia. Heat loss occurs through radiation (55–65%), conduction (10–15% increased in water), convection, respiration, and evaporation.
keyPoints: Individuals with extensive cold exposure, such as military personnel, hunters, sailors, skiers, and climbers, are at high risk.
Medications like ethanol, antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, benzodiazepines reduce vasoconstriction and thermogenesis.
Heat loss mechanisms: radiation (55–65%), conduction (10–15% increased in water), convection, respiration, evaporation.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.title: Endocrine and Neurologic Contributions to Hypothermia
items.content: Endocrine dysfunctions such as hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, and hypopituitarism increase susceptibility to hypothermia. Neurologic injuries and conditions like Shapiro's syndrome also contribute by impairing thermoregulation.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism reduces metabolic rate and impairs thermogenesis.
Adrenal insufficiency and hypopituitarism increase hypothermia risk.
Neurologic injuries disrupt shivering and vasoconstrictive responses.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.title: Understanding Thermoregulation and Hypothermia Response
items.content: Thermoregulation is primarily controlled by the preoptic anterior hypothalamus, utilizing both autonomic nervous system (ANS) and endocrine responses. The ANS initiates immediate defense mechanisms such as norepinephrine release, muscle tone increase, shivering, reflex vasoconstriction, and thermogenesis to maintain core temperature. Prolonged cold exposure activates the thyroid axis for delayed metabolic rate adjustment.
keyPoints: The preoptic anterior hypothalamus orchestrates thermo-regulation.
Immediate defense mechanisms include norepinephrine release, increased muscle tone, shivering, and reflex vasoconstriction.
Delayed control involves endocrine system activation, particularly the thyroid axis.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · THERMOREGULATION | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Management of Hypothermia
items.content: Hypothermia diagnosis is often straightforward with a clear history of cold exposure, but in urban settings, differential diagnoses should be considered. Clinical manifestations vary widely; tachycardia disproportionate to core temperature suggests secondary causes like hypoglycemia or toxin overdose. Persistent hyperventilation may indicate CNS lesions or acidosis. In hypothermic cardiac arrest, CPR is indicated unless specific conditions are met, and prognosis worsens with potassium >12 mmol/L, pH <6.5, or fibrinogen <0.5 g/L.
keyPoints: Diagnosis of hypothermia can be subtle in urban settings; consider other disease processes.
Tachycardia disproportionate to core temperature suggests secondary causes like hypoglycemia or toxin overdose.
Persistent hyperventilation may indicate CNS lesions or acidosis.
In cardiac arrest, CPR is indicated unless specific conditions are met (e.g., do-not-resuscitate status).
Prognosis worsens with potassium >12 mmol/L, pH <6.5, or fibrinogen <0.5 g/L.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · THERMOREGULATION | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Core Temperature Measurement in Hypothermia Diagnosis
items.content: Hypothermia is confirmed by measuring core temperature, ideally using a probe placed in the lower third of the esophagus for continuous monitoring. Rectal or bladder measurements may lag behind actual core changes.
keyPoints: Use esophageal probe for accurate core temperature measurement.
Continuous monitoring essential; rectal/bladder lags behind core changes.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.title: Perfusion Assessment in Hypothermic Patients
items.content: In hypothermia, pulses may be undetectable even if perfusing. Maximal monitor amplification aids detection. Echocardiography and end-tidal CO2 monitoring confirm perfusion status.
keyPoints: Pulses may be undetectable in perfusing patients; use maximal monitor amplification.
Echocardiography and ETCO2 confirm perfusion status.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.title: Management of Coagulopathies in Hypothermia
items.content: Coagulopathies are common due to cold inhibiting enzymatic reactions and impairing platelet function. Normal coagulation studies may not reflect true in vivo status.
keyPoints: Cold inhibits intrinsic cascade activation; platelet function impaired.
Normal lab results can be misleading; tests performed at 37°C.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.title: When to Use Passive vs Active Rewarming
items.content: Passive external rewarming is suitable for previously healthy patients with mild primary accidental hypothermia, provided they have sufficient glycogen reserves. It involves insulating the patient in a warm environment and covers the head, achieving a rewarming rate of 0.5°C to 2°C per hour (1.10°F to 4.4°F). Active rewarming is necessary for patients with core temperatures below 32°C (<90°F), cardiovascular instability, age extremes, CNS dysfunction, hormone insufficiency, or suspicion of secondary hypothermia.
keyPoints: Passive external rewarming rate: 0.5°C to 2°C per hour (1.10°F to 4.4°F)
Indications for passive rewarming: previously healthy patients with mild primary accidental hypothermia
Indications for active rewarming: core temperature <32°C (<90°F), cardiovascular instability, age extremes, CNS dysfunction, hormone insufficiency, suspicion of secondary hypothermia
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · REWARMING STRATEGIES

items.title: Active Core Rewarming Techniques and Considerations
items.content: Active core rewarming can be achieved through airway warming with heated humidified oxygen (40°–45°C [104°–113°F]), which adds 1°–2°C to the overall rewarming rate. Crystalloids should be heated to 40°–42°C, but are significant only during massive volume resuscitation. The most efficient method is using a countercurrent in-line heat exchanger. Gastrointestinal or bladder irrigation transfers minimal heat and is reserved for cardiac arrest patients. Closed thoracic lavage is more efficient than peritoneal lavage in severely hypothermic patients with cardiac arrest.
keyPoints: Airway rewarming temperature: 40°–45°C (104°–113°F)
Additional rewarming rate from airway warming: 1°–2°C
Crystalloid heating temperature: 40°–42°C (104°–108°F)
Efficient core rewarming method: countercurrent in-line heat exchanger
Closed thoracic lavage efficiency: more efficient than peritoneal lavage for cardiac arrest
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · REWARMING STRATEGIES

items.title: Medication Management in Hypothermic Patients
items.content: In hypothermic patients, medications generally have minimal effects on target organs and the cardiovascular system below 30°C (86°F). This is due to increased drug binding to proteins and impaired metabolism and excretion. Therefore, either a lower dose or longer interval between doses should be used to avoid toxicity during rewarming.
keyPoints: Medications are generally withheld below 30°C (86°F)
Increased binding of drugs to proteins
Impaired drug metabolism and excretion
Lower dose or longer intervals needed to prevent toxicity
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia

items.title: Management of Hypotension in Hypothermic Patients
items.content: In hypothermic patients, achieving a mean arterial pressure of at least 60 mmHg is crucial. If hypotension persists despite crystalloid/colloid infusion and rewarming, low-dose dopamine (2–5 µg/kg per min) or IV nitroglycerin may be considered to improve perfusion.
keyPoints: Target mean arterial pressure of at least 60 mmHg
Consider low-dose dopamine (2–5 µg/kg per min) if hypotension persists
IV nitroglycerin can help with vasoconstricted cardiovascular system
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia

items.title: Recognizing Frostbite Severity: Key Clinical Signs and Symptoms
items.content: Understanding the clinical presentation of frostbite is crucial for determining its severity. Initial symptoms may appear benign, but key indicators such as sensory deficiency in light touch, pain, or temperature perception are always present. The acral areas and distal extremities are most commonly affected. Deep frostbitten tissue can have a waxy, mottled, yellow, or violaceous-white appearance. Superficial frostbite is characterized by anesthesia and erythema without tissue loss, while deeper involvement may show vesiculation with edema and erythema. Hemorrhagic vesicles indicate severe frostbite and potential for significant tissue damage.
keyPoints: Sensory deficiency in light touch, pain, or temperature perception is always present.
Acral areas and distal extremities are most commonly affected.
Deep frostbitten tissue can appear waxy, mottled, yellow, or violaceous-white.
Superficial frostbite causes anesthesia and erythema without tissue loss.
Vesiculation with edema and erythema implies deeper involvement.
Hemorrhagic vesicles indicate severe frostbite.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · FROSTBITE | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Grading Frostbite: Clinical Classification Based on Cyanosis Location
items.content: Frostbite can be graded based on the location of cyanosis, which helps in assessing the severity and potential for tissue loss. Grade 1 frostbite shows no cyanosis, indicating superficial involvement. Grade 2 involves cyanosis at the distal phalanx, while grade 3 extends to the metacarpophalangeal (MP) joint. Grade 4 frostbite is characterized by cyanosis proximal to the MP joint and suggests severe injury with a higher risk of amputation.
keyPoints: Grade 1: Absence of cyanosis, indicating superficial involvement.
Grade 2: Cyanosis on the distal phalanx.
Grade 3: Cyanosis up to the metacarpophalangeal (MP) joint.
Grade 4: Cyanosis proximal to the MP joint, suggesting severe injury.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · FROSTBITE | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.question: Which group is particularly vulnerable to hypothermia due to diminished thermal perception and increased susceptibility to immobility, malnutrition, and systemic illnesses?
items.explanation: The elderly have diminished thermal perception and are more susceptible to immobility, malnutrition, and systemic illnesses that interfere with heat generation or conservation.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.question: What is a common cause of hypothermia in neonates?
options: High rates of heat loss due to increased surface-to-mass ratio
items.explanation: Neonates have high rates of heat loss because of their increased surface-to-mass ratio and lack of effective shivering and adaptive behavioral responses.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.question: Which substance causes vasodilation, reducing thermogenesis and potentially impairing judgment?
items.explanation: Ethanol causes vasodilation (which increases heat loss), reduces thermogenesis and gluconeogenesis, and may impair judgment or lead to obtundation.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.question: Which condition is associated with hypothermia due to neuroglycopenic effects on hypothalamic function?
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia, most commonly caused by insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents, is associated with hypothermia, in part because of neuroglycopenic effects on hypothalamic function.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.question: Which autonomic nervous system response contributes to thermogenesis in cold environments?
items.explanation: Autonomic nervous system responses include increased muscle tone and shivering, leading to thermogenesis.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · THERMOREGULATION | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What does persistent hyperventilation in a hypothermic patient suggest?
options: Central nervous system lesion or organic acidosis
items.explanation: Persistent hyperventilation suggests a central nervous system lesion or an organic acidosis, not a normal response.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · THERMOREGULATION | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What is paradoxical undressing in the context of hypothermia?
options: Inappropriate removal of clothing due to cold stress
items.explanation: Paradoxical undressing is the inappropriate removal of clothing in response to a cold stress.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · THERMOREGULATION | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which method is preferable for measuring core temperature in hypothermic patients?
options: Placement of a probe in the lower third of the esophagus
items.explanation: Esophageal placement is preferable for core temperature measurement.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.question: What should be considered if a hypothermic patient has no perfusion and a core temperature below 30°C?
options: Attempt up to three maximal power resuscitations
items.explanation: Up to three attempts at maximal power should be considered before rewarming.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.question: Which solution is preferred for intravenous or intraosseous administration in hypothermic patients?
items.explanation: Normal saline is preferred due to inefficient lactate metabolism.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.question: Why should arterial blood gases not be corrected for temperature in hypothermic patients?
options: Because pH and PCO₂ values are unaffected by temperature changes
To avoid misinterpretation of acid-base balance
Due to the inefficiency of bicarbonate buffering at low temperatures
items.explanation: Uncorrected values reflect appropriate ventilation and balance.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.question: Which of the following conditions necessitate active rewarming rather than passive external rewarming?
options: Core temperature <32°C (<90°F), cardiovascular instability, age extremes, CNS dysfunction, hormone insufficiency, and suspicion of secondary hypothermia.
Mild primary accidental hypothermia in previously healthy patients with sufficient glycogen stores.
Chronic severe hypothermia without any signs of cardiovascular instability or CNS dysfunction.
Acute mild primary accidental hypothermia with adequate endogenous thermogenesis.
items.explanation: Active rewarming is necessary for core temperature <32°C, cardiovascular instability, age extremes, CNS dysfunction, hormone insufficiency, and suspicion of secondary hypothermia.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · REWARMING STRATEGIES

items.question: Which method should be avoided when applying heat to the extremities of patients with chronic severe hypothermia?
options: Application of heat directly to the extremities.
Heated irrigation of the gastrointestinal tract.
items.explanation: Applying heat directly to the extremities can induce peripheral vasodilation and precipitate core temperature afterdrop in chronic severe hypothermia.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · REWARMING STRATEGIES

items.question: Which of the following is a recommended method for active external rewarming?
options: Heated humidified oxygen via mask or endotracheal tube.
items.explanation: Active external rewarming is best accomplished with forced-air heating blankets and airway rewarming using heated humidified oxygen.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · REWARMING STRATEGIES

items.question: At what core body temperature are medications generally withheld?
items.explanation: Medications are typically withheld below 30°C (86°F) due to altered drug metabolism and increased binding.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia

items.question: What is the recommended initial management for atrial arrhythmias in hypothermic patients?
items.explanation: Atrial arrhythmias should be monitored initially without intervention as they often convert spontaneously during rewarming.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia

items.question: Which medication is considered potentially hazardous for euthyroid patients with hypothermia?
items.explanation: The administration of parenteral levothyroxine to euthyroid patients with hypothermia is potentially hazardous.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is always present in the initial presentation of frostbite?
options: Sensory deficiency affecting light touch, pain, or temperature perception
items.explanation: The initial presentation always includes a sensory deficiency affecting light touch, pain, or temperature perception.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · FROSTBITE | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What does the appearance of hemorrhagic vesicles in frostbite indicate?
options: Serious injury to the microvasculature and severe frostbite
items.explanation: Hemorrhagic vesicles reflect a serious injury to the microvasculature and indicate severe frostbite.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · FROSTBITE | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common nonfreezing peripheral cold injury?
items.explanation: Deep frostbite with tissue loss is a freezing injury, not a nonfreezing peripheral cold injury.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · FROSTBITE | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.statement: Primary accidental hypothermia is only prevalent in winter months and colder climates.
items.explanation: Although most cases occur in the winter months and in colder climates, primary accidental hypothermia is surprisingly common in warmer regions as well.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.statement: Increased peripheral-blood flow due to skin diseases can lead to excessive heat loss.
items.explanation: With extensive burns, psoriasis, erythrodermas, and other skin diseases, increased peripheral-blood flow leads to excessive heat loss.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.statement: A core temperature below 10–12°C (50–54°F) may preclude continuing resuscitation.
items.explanation: Core temperatures <10–12°C are considered a factor that may preclude continued resuscitation efforts.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · THERMOREGULATION | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: A patient with hypothermia will always present with tachycardia.
items.explanation: Tachycardia disproportionate to core temperature suggests secondary causes, not a direct result of hypothermia.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · THERMOREGULATION | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: Pulse oximetry is reliable in hypothermic patients with vasoconstriction.
items.explanation: Pulse oximetry is often unreliable due to vasoconstriction.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.statement: Hyperkalemia can occur in hypothermic patients.
items.explanation: Hyperkalemia is possible despite being less common than hypokalemia.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.statement: Electric blankets are recommended for patients with vasoconstricted skin during hypothermia treatment.
items.explanation: Electric blankets should be avoided because vasoconstricted skin is easily burned.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · REWARMING STRATEGIES

items.statement: Heated irrigation of the gastrointestinal tract efficiently transfers heat in hypothermic patients.
items.explanation: Heated irrigation of the gastrointestinal tract transfers minimal heat due to limited surface area.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · REWARMING STRATEGIES

items.statement: Shaking rigors from infection may be mistaken for shivering in hypothermic patients.
items.explanation: Infection symptoms like shaking rigors can mimic shivering, which is a response to cold.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia

items.statement: Empirical antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for all hypothermic patients.
items.explanation: Empirical antibiotics are most warranted in the elderly, neonates, and immunocompromised unless an infectious source is identified.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia

items.statement: Chilblain (pernio) results from exposure to damp cold above the freezing point.
items.explanation: Chilblain results from neuronal and endothelial damage induced by repetitive exposure to damp cold above the freezing point.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · FROSTBITE | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.explanation: Superficial frostbite does not entail tissue loss but rather causes only anesthesia and erythema.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · FROSTBITE | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.assertion: Ethanol consumption can lead to hypothermia.
items.reason: Ethanol causes vasodilation, which increases heat loss.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES
items.assertion: Neurologic injury increases susceptibility to hypothermia.
items.reason: Agenesis of the corpus callosum leads to increased heat production.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; agenesis of the corpus callosum causes episodic hypothermia due to a rapid fall in temperature after perspiration.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries | HYPOTHERMIA | CAUSES

items.assertion: Coagulopathies are common in hypothermic patients.
items.reason: Cold inhibits enzymatic reactions required for coagulation cascade activation.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.assertion: Arterial blood gases should be corrected for temperature in hypothermic patients.
items.reason: Temperature affects pH and PCO₂ values, leading to misinterpretation if not corrected.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; arterial blood gases should not be corrected.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · DIAGNOSIS AND STABILIZATION

items.assertion: The development of mummification in frostbite can take weeks to months.
items.reason: Ice-crystal formation distorts and destroys cellular architecture, leading to progressive dermal ischemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the direct explanation for the time it takes for mummification to develop.
items.reference: Hypothermia and Peripheral Cold Injuries · FROSTBITE | CLINICAL PRESENTATION
